sources_audited.note: The Census page directly defines 813920 as organizations promoting professional interests through research, statistics, standards, lobbying, and publications, and gives the stated cross-references excluding business associations and standalone lobbyists.

The BLS May 2023 page is explicitly for broader NAICS 813900 and reports the cited employment shares: management 19.84%, business and financial operations 28.07%, office and administrative support 20.84%, and arts/media 5.56%. The packet clearly labels the exact-813920 exposure mapping as a proxy.

The GrowthZone release reports more than 1,000 U.S. and Canadian association/chamber respondents, 41% association membership growth, nearly half calling AI critical, and budget and staff-capacity barriers. It does not measure production deployment, labor substitution, exact-813920 organizations, or retained AI benefit.

The IRS page states that professional associations are business leagues and that qualifying business leagues are not organized for profit and may not distribute net earnings to private shareholders or individuals. This supports the governance and private-inurement premise, not the numerical eligibility or transfer estimates themselves.

The Chicago Booth article states that about 1% of U.S. nonprofits merge or are acquired in a year and discusses stigma, succession, resource constraints, mission alignment, and board involvement. It does not isolate professional organizations or establish that all counted combinations are qualifying control transfers under this screen.

The ASAE release reports an Oxford Economics study of 20,000 associations and the cited annual event scale of more than 272,000 events and nearly 52 million participants. It establishes current sector scale but does not directly support a five-year quantity path or the share that will still require an operator.

The report supports the cited median renewal rate of 85%, five-year membership growth for 49% and decline for 29% of respondents, and networking, continuing education/certification, and specialized information as leading join reasons. These association survey measures remain indirect proxies for retained AI benefit, constant-quality service quantity, and exact-813920 demand.

caveats: The a estimate maps broad 813900 occupational shares to task exposure; the source includes labor, business, political, and other organizations and does not measure task hours or feasible AI substitution, although the packet discloses this limitation and uses a broad PROXY range.

The rho base translates vendor-sponsored stated AI importance into implementation of exposed workflows. S3 measures sentiment and barriers, not governed production deployment or verified labor substitution; the PROXY label and wide range are honest.

The s5 estimate converts an all-nonprofit annual merger-or-acquisition statistic into an exact-813920 five-year qualifying-control probability. The source does not distinguish affiliations or asset transfers from continuing-organization control changes, making firm availability especially uncertain even though the bridge discounts and discloses the mismatch.

The q and d5 bridges use membership renewal and respondent-reported membership change as proxies for retained AI benefit and constant-price, constant-quality service quantity. The numerical survey claims are supported, but neither transformation is observed directly.
S6 documents current association event scale, not future demand growth or operator-required demand; the o primitive is appropriately labeled ESTIMATE but remains weakly anchored.

The packet marks the lens as not heterogeneous even while acknowledging materially different labor mixes across learned societies, bar associations, health-professional bodies, and other professional organizations. This weakens interpretation of the single base case but does not alter the already broad tier interval or MODEL_CONDITIONED action.

Eligibility e discounts captive governance and standalone transferability while s5 separately discounts member consent and control-transfer constraints. This introduces some downward overlap between e and s5, but correcting that overlap within the registered ranges would not change the PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.
summary: Mechanics pass. The memo follows the registered evidence, uses a coherent default lens, and is candid that a nonprofit-heavy, member-governed sector has attractive information-work exposure but scarce qualifying control transfers. Every cited source supports its stated factual premise, though the most decision-relevant primitives rely on broad association or nonprofit proxies and disclosed judgment. Those weaknesses warrant caveated publication, not rejection: evidence states are honest, ranges are wide, the action remains MODEL_CONDITIONED, and no identified correction or evidence downgrade is shown to change the base tier or action category.
